curriculum_source: CFA Program 2027 Level III, Volume 5 (Ethical and Professional Standards), Learning Module 3: Guidance for Standard II: Integrity of Capital Markets

reading.reading_name: Guidance for Standard II: Integrity of Capital Markets

drills.los_text: demonstrate a thorough knowledge of the CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct by interpreting the Code and Standards in various situations involving issues of professional integrity
drills.reading_name: Guidance for Standard II: Integrity of Capital Markets

questions.stem: *Example 1 Barnes/Staple/Halsey*: Barnes (president/controlling shareholder of SmartTown) accepts tender offer at nearly double market price; tells sister → niece → husband Staple → broker Halsey; Halsey immediately buys SmartTown stock. *Most likely*:

options.A: information regarding pending sale is both material and nonpublic.
options.B: no violation.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: information regarding pending sale is both material and nonpublic; Staple violated Standard II(A) by communicating inside information to his broker; Halsey also violated the standard by buying the shares on the basis of material nonpublic information.

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment: 'The information regarding the pending sale is both material and nonpublic. Staple violated Standard II(A) by communicating the inside information to his broker. Halsey also violated the standard by buying the shares on the basis of material nonpublic information.'
rationales.B: Both Staple and Halsey violate.
rationales.C: Both violate.

questions.stem: *Example 2 Peter/Bright Ideas*: assisting secondary offering; during video call learns earnings projections have significantly dropped; Scotland and Pierce salespeople/PMs walk in/out of his office and hear; firm personnel trade the stock before call concludes. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because Peter did not himself trade on the earnings information.
options.B: Peter violated Standard II(A) because he failed to prevent the transfer and misuse of material nonpublic information to others in his firm.
options.C: Only the salespeople and portfolio managers who overheard the call bear responsibility under II(A).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Peter violated II(A) by failing to prevent the transfer and misuse of MNPI within his firm.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Peter violated Standard II(A) because he failed to prevent the transfer and misuse of material nonpublic information to others in his firm; Peter's firm should have adopted information barriers to prevent communication of nonpublic information between departments; firm personnel who are members or candidates and who traded on the information also violated Standard II(A) by trading on inside information.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'Peter violated Standard II(A) because he failed to prevent the transfer and misuse of material nonpublic information to others in his firm. Peter's firm should have adopted information barriers to prevent the communication of nonpublic information between departments of the firm. Firm personnel who are members or candidates and who traded on the information also violated Standard II(A) by trading on inside information.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Peter's failure to contain the information is itself the violation; the traders' misuse compounds it.

questions.stem: *Example 3 Levenson*: invited with 10 largest shareholders to meet finance director in Taipei; director says workforce expected to strike next Friday and cripple production. Can Levenson use to change rating? *Most likely*:

options.A: yes — she was told.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: Levenson must first determine whether material information is public.

rationales.A: Small group ≠ public.
rationales.B: Not disseminated.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Levenson must first determine whether material information is public; according to Standard II(A), if company has not made this information public (a small group forum does NOT qualify as a method of public dissemination), she must NOT use the information.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment: 'Levenson must first determine whether the material information is public. According to Standard II(A), if the company has not made this information public (a small group forum does not qualify as a method of public dissemination), she must not use the information.'

questions.stem: *Example 4 Fechtman*: considering hold/sell of oil & gas exploration company; her doctor (who casually follows markets) mentions belief that company will soon be bought by multinational conglomerate. *Most likely*:

options.A: although information on an expected takeover bid may be generally material and nonpublic, in this case the SOURCE of information is UNRELIABLE, so the information cannot be considered material.
options.B: Fechtman cannot trade because takeover information is always material and nonpublic.
options.C: Fechtman may trade only after asking the company to confirm or deny the takeover rumor.

rationales.A:
Full statement: although information on an expected takeover bid may be generally material and nonpublic, in this case the SOURCE of information is UNRELIABLE, so the information cannot be considered material; therefore, Fechtman is not prohibited from trading the stock based on this information.

Correct. Per Example 4 Comment: 'Although information on an expected takeover bid may be generally material and nonpublic, in this case, the source of information is unreliable, so the information cannot be considered material. Therefore, Fechtman is not prohibited from trading the stock based on this information.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Here the SOURCE — a doctor casually following markets — is unreliable, so the tip lacks the reliability needed for materiality.
rationales.C: Incorrect. No confirmation step is required; the unreliable casual source means the information is not material, and she may act on her own analysis.

questions.stem: *Example 5 Teja/SFC (mosaic theory)*: buy-side analyst studies financial reports, visits operations, talks to designers and retailers; concludes SFC's extravagant new designs are not selling well and next-quarter earnings will drop; issues sell recommendation. *Most likely*:

options.A: Teja violated Standard II(A) because his conclusion anticipated material quarterly earnings information.
options.B: while company information on quarterly earnings data is material and nonpublic, Teja based his conclusion about the earnings drop on public information and pieces of nonmaterial nonpublic information (such as opinions of designers and retailers).
options.C: Teja may publish his conclusion only after the company releases its quarterly earnings.

rationales.A: Incorrect. His conclusion was assembled from public information and nonmaterial pieces — the mosaic theory permits acting on it.
rationales.B:
Full statement: while company information on quarterly earnings data is material and nonpublic, Teja based his conclusion about the earnings drop on public information and pieces of nonmaterial nonpublic information (such as opinions of designers and retailers); therefore, trading based on Teja's correct conclusion is not prohibited by Standard II(A).

Correct. Per Example 5 Comment: 'While company information on quarterly earnings data is material and nonpublic, Teja based his conclusion about the earnings drop on public information and pieces of nonmaterial nonpublic information (such as opinions of designers and retailers). Therefore, trading based on Teja's correct conclusion is not prohibited by Standard II(A).'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Mosaic-based conclusions may be acted on and published without waiting for the company's release.

questions.stem: *Example 6 Clement/Turgot (mosaic theory)*: senior automobile analyst speaks with reps, salespeople, labor leaders, APAC currency analysts, banking officials; concludes new model won't meet expectations, restructuring may face union opposition, KRW depreciation pressures margins, banks may take tougher stance; retains support material. Changes rating outperform → underperform. *Most likely*:

options.A: Clement violated Standard II(A) because conversations with company representatives conveyed inside knowledge.
options.B: Clement's conclusion is unusable because some of his sources were nonpublic conversations.
options.C: to reach conclusion about the value of the company, Clement pieced together a number of nonmaterial or public bits of information that affect Turgot Chariots.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Each piece he gathered was nonmaterial or public; assembling them into a valuable conclusion is the mosaic theory at work.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Nonpublic conversations are fine when the information conveyed is nonmaterial; the mosaic conclusion may be used.
rationales.C:
Full statement: to reach conclusion about the value of the company, Clement pieced together a number of nonmaterial or public bits of information that affect Turgot Chariots; therefore, under the mosaic theory, Clement did NOT violate Standard II(A) in drafting the report.

Correct. Per Example 6 Comment: 'To reach a conclusion about the value of the company, Clement pieced together a number of nonmaterial or public bits of information that affect Turgot Chariots. Therefore, under the mosaic theory, Clement did not violate Standard II(A) in drafting the report.'

questions.stem: *Example 7 Clement/Zito (analyst recommendation as MNPI)*: while preparing for TV interview, Clement mentions to producers and Zito the info he will discuss; Zito sells Turgot holdings and phones her father. *Most likely*:

options.A: when Zito receives advance notice of Clement's change of opinion, she knows it will have a material impact on the stock price, even if she is not aware of Clement's underlying reasoning.
options.B: There is no violation because Zito did not know the substance of Clement's revised opinion.
options.C: Only Clement violated the standard by mentioning his upcoming interview topics to outsiders.

rationales.A:
Full statement: when Zito receives advance notice of Clement's change of opinion, she knows it will have a material impact on the stock price, even if she is not aware of Clement's underlying reasoning; she is not a client of Clement but obtains early access to material nonpublic information prior to publication; her trades are based on material nonpublic information, thus she violated Standard II(A); Zito further violated the standard by relaying the information to her father — it would NOT matter if he or any other family member traded; the act of PROVIDING the information violated Standard II(A).

Correct. Per Example 7 Comment: 'When Zito receives advance notice of Clement's change of opinion, she knows it will have a material impact on the stock price, even if she is not aware of Clement's underlying reasoning. She is not a client of Clement but obtains early access to the material nonpublic information prior to publication. Her trades are based on material nonpublic information, thus she violated Standard II(A). Zito further violated the standard by relaying the information to her father. It would not matter if he or any other family member traded; the act of providing the information violated Standard II(A).'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Advance notice that a prominent analyst's opinion is changing is itself material nonpublic information; trading on it violates II(A).
rationales.C: Incorrect. Zito's trading on the advance notice is the direct violation; Clement's disclosure created the exposure but does not absolve her.

questions.stem: *Example 8 Kellogg/National*: retired investment professional owns shares; close friend Mayfield (senior executive at National) tells him bank will surprise investment community with excellent earnings; Kellogg (believing Mayfield knows the law) doubles his position; stock later drops 60% on combined news. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because Kellogg ultimately lost money on the additional shares.
options.B: even though Kellogg believes that Mayfield would not break the law by disclosing inside information, Kellogg violated Standard II(A) by purchasing additional shares of National.
options.C: There is no violation because Kellogg reasonably trusted that Mayfield would not disclose inside information.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Losing money on the trade does not absolve trading on MNPI.
rationales.B:
Full statement: even though Kellogg believes that Mayfield would not break the law by disclosing inside information, Kellogg violated Standard II(A) by purchasing additional shares of National; the fact that he lost money on the purchase does NOT absolve Kellogg for trading on material nonpublic information; it is the member's or candidate's responsibility to make sure, before executing investment actions, that comments about earnings are not material nonpublic information.

Correct. Per Example 8 Comment: 'Even though Kellogg believes that Mayfield would not break the law by disclosing inside information, Kellogg violated Standard II(A) by purchasing additional shares of National. The fact that he lost money on the purchase does not absolve Kellogg for trading on material nonpublic information. It is the member's or candidate's responsibility to make sure, before executing investment actions, that comments about earnings are not material nonpublic information.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Kellogg's trust in his friend is irrelevant; a senior executive's earnings preview is MNPI, and purchasing on it violates II(A).

questions.stem: *Example 9 Nadler*: gets text from another firm's trader (known for years) saying he believes software company will report strong earnings in two days; Nadler has independent buy order from PM and completes it aggressively before earnings. *Most likely*:

options.A: violates II(A).
options.B: no framework.
options.C: there are often rumors and 'whisper numbers' before release of any kind.

rationales.A: Rumor is market noise.
rationales.B: Market noise.
rationales.C:
Full statement: there are often rumors and 'whisper numbers' before release of any kind; text message from the other trader would most likely be considered market noise; unless Nadler knew that the trader had an ongoing business relationship with the public firm, he had no reason to suspect he was receiving material nonpublic information that would prevent him from completing the trading request of the portfolio manager.

Correct. Per Example 9 Comment: 'There are often rumors and whisper numbers before a release of any kind. The text message from the other trader would most likely be considered market noise. Unless Nadler knew that the trader had an ongoing business relationship with the public firm, he had no reason to suspect he was receiving material nonpublic information that would prevent him from completing the trading request of the portfolio manager.'

questions.stem: *Example 10 McCoy*: senior drug analyst; firm hires service connecting her to cancer treatment experts; enhances her understanding of latest therapies via phone conversations. *Most likely*:

options.A: McCoy is appropriately using expert networks to enhance her evaluation process.
options.B: prohibited.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: McCoy is appropriately using expert networks to enhance her evaluation process; she neither asked for nor received information that may be considered material and nonpublic (such as preliminary trial results); McCoy is allowed to seek advice from professionals within the industry that she follows.

Correct. Per Example 10 Comment: 'McCoy is appropriately using the expert networks to enhance her evaluation process. She neither asked for nor received information that may be considered material and nonpublic, such as preliminary trial results. McCoy is allowed to seek advice from professionals within the industry that she follows.'
rationales.B: Expert networks acceptable.
rationales.C: OK use.

questions.stem: *Example 11 Watson*: hedge fund analyst relies on outside experts arranged through networks with MNPI-deterrence policies; on call scientist leading semiconductor tests expresses disappointment with new product performance; Watson relays to others at fund, who sell current position and buy put options. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because Watson relied on an expert network whose agreements include MNPI-deterrence policies.
options.B: Watson violated Standard II(A) by passing along material nonpublic information concerning the ongoing product tests, which the fund used to trade in the securities and options of the related company.
options.C: Only the scientist violated the standard; Watson may freely use whatever an expert consultant volunteers.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Network agreements do not discharge the analyst's own duty to assess whether information received is material and nonpublic.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Watson violated Standard II(A) by passing along material nonpublic information concerning the ongoing product tests, which the fund used to trade in the securities and options of the related company; Watson cannot simply rely on the agreements signed by individuals who participate in expert networks that state that he has not received information that would prohibit his trading activity; he must make his own determination whether information he received through these arrangements reaches a materiality threshold that would influence his trading.

Correct. Per Example 11 Comment: 'Watson violated Standard II(A) by passing along material nonpublic information concerning the ongoing product tests, which the fund used to trade in the securities and options of the related company. Watson cannot simply rely on the agreements signed by individuals who participate in expert networks that state that he has not received information that would prohibit his trading activity. He must make his own determination whether information he received through these arrangements reaches a materiality threshold that would influence his trading.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Watson violated II(A) by passing along MNPI that the fund then traded on; recipients bear responsibility for their own use.

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 3 Andrews/Wright*: hearing rumors of possible merger; Wright mentions during interview that his firm is acting as adviser; no public releases. Least likely violation *most likely*:

options.A: Wait until next day before trading.
options.B: Notify all investment managers so no clients disadvantaged.
options.C: placing the securities mentioned as part of the merger on the firm's restricted trading list.

rationales.A: Restricted list is right approach.
rationales.B: Restricted list.
rationales.C:
Full statement: placing the securities mentioned as part of the merger on the firm's restricted trading list; guidance for Standard II(A) recommends adding securities to firm's restricted list when firm has or may have material nonpublic information; by adding these securities to this list, Andrews would uphold this standard; waiting until next day will not ensure news of the merger is made public — negotiations may take longer or merger may never happen — Andrews must wait until information is disseminated to market; should NOT disclose information to other managers, no trading allowed on MNPI.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 3 Solution: 'The guidance for Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information recommends adding securities to the firm's restricted list when the firm has or may have material nonpublic information. By adding these securities to this list, Andrews would uphold this standard. Because waiting until the next day will not ensure that news of the merger is made public, A is incorrect... B is incorrect because Andrews should not disclose the information to other managers; no trading is allowed on material nonpublic information.'

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 4 Rodriguez/Hart*: CFO of Mega Retail tells long-time friend Hart during golf that quarter will be almost 10% above estimates; Hart buys large stake in local exchange-traded retail fund (not Mega directly). *Most likely*:

options.A: Hart violated Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information.
options.B: no violation — retail fund not Mega.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Hart violated Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information; Hart's decision to invest in retail fund appears directly correlated with Rodriguez's statement about the successful quarter of Mega Retail; Rodriguez's information would be considered material because it would influence share price of Mega Retail and probably influence price of entire exchange-traded retail fund; Rodriguez also shared MNPI — sharing of such information in conversation between friends also violates Standard II(A).

Correct. Per Practice Problem 4 Solution: 'Hart's decision to invest in the retail fund appears directly correlated with Rodriguez's statement about the successful quarter of Mega Retail and thus violates Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information. Rodriguez's information would be considered material because it would influence the share price of Mega Retail and probably influence the price of the entire exchange-traded retail fund. Thus, B is incorrect. C is also incorrect because Rodriguez shared information that was both material and nonpublic. Company officers regularly have such knowledge about their firms, which is not a violation. The sharing of such information, however, even in a conversation between friends, does violate Standard II(A).'
rationales.B: Both violate — even through fund.
rationales.C: MNPI through ETF.

questions.stem: *Materiality — reliability of source* *most likely*:

options.A: Materiality is judged solely by the substance of the information, without regard to who provided it.
options.B: in addition to substance and specificity of information, source or relative reliability of information also determines materiality.
options.C: Information from any identified source is automatically material once it is specific.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The source's reliability is itself a determinant of materiality alongside substance and specificity.
rationales.B:
Full statement: in addition to substance and specificity of information, source or relative reliability of information also determines materiality; less reliable a source, less likely information provided would be considered material; factual information from corporate insider regarding significant new contract is likely to be material, whereas assumption based on speculation by competitor about same contract is likely to be less reliable and, therefore, not material; information about trials of new drug/product/service under development from qualified personnel involved in trials is likely material; educated conjecture by subject experts NOT connected to the trials is unlikely to be material.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance on What Is 'Material' Information: 'In addition to the substance and specificity of the information, the source or relative reliability of the information also determines materiality. The less reliable a source, the less likely the information provided would be considered material. For example, factual information from a corporate insider regarding a significant new contract for a company is likely to be material, whereas an assumption based on speculation by a competitor about the same contract is likely to be less reliable and, therefore, not material. Additionally, information about trials of a new drug, product, or service under development from qualified personnel involved in the trials is likely to be material, whereas educated conjecture by subject experts not connected to the trials is unlikely to be material.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Specificity alone does not establish materiality; an unreliable source can render information non-material.

questions.stem: *Ambiguity of information's price effect* *most likely*:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: the more ambiguous the effect of the information on price, the LESS material that information is considered.

rationales.A: Ambiguity reduces materiality.
rationales.B: Ambiguity + time.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the more ambiguous the effect of the information on price, the LESS material that information is considered; if it is unclear whether and to what extent the information will affect price of a security, the information may not be considered material; passage of time may also render information that was once important immaterial.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance: 'Also, the more ambiguous the effect of the information on price, the less material that information is considered. If it is unclear whether and to what extent the information will affect the price of a security, the information may not be considered material. The passage of time may also render information that was once important immaterial.'

questions.stem: *Nonpublic — small group forum* *most likely*:

options.A: information is 'nonpublic' until it has been disseminated or is available to the marketplace in general (as opposed to a select group of investors).
options.B: Information becomes public the moment it is shared beyond the issuing company, regardless of audience size.
options.C: Disclosure to a room of professional investors at a closed conference constitutes public dissemination.

rationales.A:
Full statement: information is 'nonpublic' until it has been disseminated or is available to the marketplace in general (as opposed to a select group of investors); 'disseminated' can be defined as 'made known' — for example, company report of profits posted on internet and distributed widely through press release or accompanied by a filing has been effectively disseminated to the marketplace; must have reasonable expectation that people have received the information before it can be considered public; is NOT necessary to wait for slowest method of delivery.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance on What Constitutes 'Nonpublic' Information: 'Information is "nonpublic" until it has been disseminated or is available to the marketplace in general (as opposed to a select group of investors). "Disseminated" can be defined as "made known." For example, a company report of profits that is posted on the internet and distributed widely through a press release or accompanied by a filing has been effectively disseminated to the marketplace. Members and candidates must have a reasonable expectation that people have received the information before it can be considered public. It is not necessary, however, to wait for the slowest method of delivery.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Sharing with a select group is not dissemination to the marketplace in general.
rationales.C: Incorrect. A closed professional audience is a select group; the information remains nonpublic.

questions.stem: *Selective disclosure by corporations* *most likely*:

options.A: Once a corporation discloses information to any analyst, members may treat that information as public.
options.B: must be particularly aware of information that is selectively disclosed by corporations to small group of investors, analysts, or other market participants.
options.C: Selective disclosure concerns apply only to statements made by company insiders trading their own shares.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Disclosure to a small group of analysts or investors is precisely the selective disclosure the guidance warns about.
rationales.B:
Full statement: must be particularly aware of information that is selectively disclosed by corporations to small group of investors, analysts, or other market participants; information that is made available to analysts remains nonpublic until it is made available to investors in general; corporations that disclose information on a limited basis create the potential for insider-trading violations; issues of selective disclosure may arise when a corporate insider provides material information to analysts in a briefing or conference call BEFORE that information is released to the public; analysts must be aware that a disclosure made to a room full of analysts does NOT necessarily make the disclosed information 'public'.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance: 'Members and candidates must be particularly aware of information that is selectively disclosed by corporations to a small group of investors, analysts, or other market participants. Information that is made available to analysts remains nonpublic until it is made available to investors in general. Corporations that disclose information on a limited basis create the potential for insider-trading violations. Issues of selective disclosure may arise when a corporate insider provides material information to analysts in a briefing or conference call before that information is released to the public. Analysts must be aware that a disclosure made to a room full of analysts does not necessarily make the disclosed information "public."'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The concern is corporate communication practices, not insider personal trading.

questions.stem: *Legitimate use of insider information* under Standard II(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Standard II(A) admits no exceptions: insider information may never be received or used for any purpose.
options.B: Due-diligence teams may use insider information received from the target for personal trading, provided the deal closes.
options.C: a member or candidate MAY use insider information provided legitimately by source company for specific purpose of conducting due diligence according to business agreement between parties for such activities as mergers, loan underwriting, credit ratings, and offering engagements.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Information provided legitimately by the source company for due diligence under a business agreement (e.g., M&A or lending) may be used for that specific purpose.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The exception is confined to the agreed diligence purpose; personal trading on it is prohibited.
rationales.C:
Full statement: a member or candidate MAY use insider information provided legitimately by source company for specific purpose of conducting due diligence according to business agreement between parties for such activities as mergers, loan underwriting, credit ratings, and offering engagements; in such instances, investment professional would NOT be considered in violation of Standard II(A) by using material information; HOWEVER, use of insider information provided by source company for OTHER purposes, especially to trade or entice others to trade the securities of the firm, is a violation of this standard.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance: 'A member or candidate may use insider information provided legitimately by the source company for the specific purpose of conducting due diligence according to the business agreement between the parties for such activities as mergers, loan underwriting, credit ratings, and offering engagements. In such instances, the investment professional would not be considered in violation of Standard II(A) by using the material information. However, the use of insider information provided by the source company for other purposes, especially to trade or entice others to trade the securities of the firm, is a violation of this standard.'

questions.stem: *Social media groups with membership limitations* *most likely*:

options.A: important for investment professionals to understand implications of using information from internet and social media platforms because all such information may not actually be considered public.
options.B: Anything posted on a social media platform is public information because it exists on the internet.
options.C: Membership-limited groups are the platform's compliance problem, not the participating analyst's.

rationales.A:
Full statement: important for investment professionals to understand implications of using information from internet and social media platforms because all such information may not actually be considered public; some social media platforms require membership in specific groups to access published content; members and candidates participating in groups with membership limitations should VERIFY that material information obtained from these sources can also be accessed from a source that would be considered available to the public BEFORE using that information.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance on Social Media: 'It is important for investment professionals to understand the implications of using information from the internet and social media platforms because all such information may not actually be considered public. Some social media platforms require membership in specific groups to access the published content. Members and candidates participating in groups with membership limitations should verify that material information obtained from these sources can also be accessed from a source that would be considered available to the public before using that information.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Information in restricted-membership groups may not be public; posting online is not dissemination to the marketplace in general.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The participating member must verify that material information from such groups is publicly available before using it.

questions.stem: *Analyst report as material information* under II(A) *most likely*:

options.A: An analyst's own pending recommendation can never be material because it is opinion rather than fact.
options.B: when a particularly well-known or respected analyst issues a report or makes changes to his or her recommendation, that information alone may influence the market and thus may be considered material.
options.C: A respected analyst's recommendation change is material only after the issuing firm confirms it publicly.

rationales.A: Incorrect. A well-known analyst's report or recommendation change may itself move the market and therefore be material.
rationales.B:
Full statement: when a particularly well-known or respected analyst issues a report or makes changes to his or her recommendation, that information alone may influence the market and thus may be considered material; the analyst is NOT a company insider, however, and does not have access to inside information; presumably, analyst created report from information available to public (mosaic theory) and by using expertise to interpret information; analyst's hard work paid for by the client generated the conclusions; simply because public in general would find conclusions material does not require analyst make his or her work public; investors who are not clients of the analyst can either do the work themselves or become clients of the analyst.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance on Investment Research Reports: 'When a particularly well-known or respected analyst issues a report or makes changes to his or her recommendation, that information alone may influence the market and thus may be considered material. The analyst is not a company insider, however, and does not have access to inside information. Presumably, the analyst created the report from information available to the public (mosaic theory) and by using his or her expertise to interpret the information. The analyst's hard work, paid for by the client, generated the conclusions. Simply because the public in general would find the conclusions material does not require that the analyst make his or her work public. Investors who are not clients of the analyst can either do the work themselves or become clients of the analyst to gain access to the analyst's expertise.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Materiality attaches to the expected market impact of the recommendation; issuer confirmation is irrelevant.

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 1 mosaic theory* *most likely* holds that an analyst:

options.A: violates the C&S if fails to have knowledge of and comply with applicable laws.
options.B: should use all available and relevant information in support of an investment recommendation.
options.C: can use material public information and nonmaterial nonpublic information in the analyst's analysis.

rationales.A: Material public + nonmaterial nonpublic.
rationales.B: Mosaic theory.
rationales.C:
Full statement: can use material public information and nonmaterial nonpublic information in the analyst's analysis; mosaic theory states that an analyst may use material public information and nonmaterial nonpublic information in creating a larger picture than shown by any individual piece of information — conclusions the analyst reaches become material only after the pieces are assembled.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 1 Solution: 'The mosaic theory states that an analyst may use material public information and nonmaterial nonpublic information in creating a larger picture than shown by any individual piece of information and the conclusions the analyst reaches become material only after the pieces are assembled. Options A and C are accurate statements relating to the Code and Standards but do not describe the mosaic theory.'

questions.stem: *Mosaic conclusion aggregation* *most likely*:

options.A: financial analyst gathers and interprets large quantities of information from many sources.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: financial analyst gathers and interprets large quantities of information from many sources; analyst may use significant conclusions derived from analysis of public and nonmaterial nonpublic information as basis for investment recommendations and decisions EVEN IF those conclusions would have been material inside information had they been communicated directly to the analyst by a company; under 'mosaic theory,' financial analysts are free to act on this collection, or mosaic, of information without risking violation.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Mosaic Theory guidance: 'A financial analyst gathers and interprets large quantities of information from many sources. The analyst may use significant conclusions derived from the analysis of public and nonmaterial nonpublic information as the basis for investment recommendations and decisions even if those conclusions would have been material inside information had they been communicated directly to the analyst by a company. Under the "mosaic theory," financial analysts are free to act on this collection, or mosaic, of information without risking violation.'
rationales.B: Conclusions from public + nonmaterial nonpublic.
rationales.C: Mosaic aggregation.

questions.stem: *Save and document research* under mosaic theory *most likely*:

options.A: Analysts using the mosaic approach should avoid keeping records, since documentation could suggest possession of MNPI.
options.B: although analysts are free to use mosaic information in their research reports, they SHOULD save and document all their research.
options.C: Documentation is required only when a regulator opens an inquiry into the analyst's conclusions.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Saving and documenting the research is the recommended protection — it demonstrates the conclusion was assembled from nonmaterial and public pieces.
rationales.B:
Full statement: although analysts are free to use mosaic information in their research reports, they SHOULD save and document all their research; evidence of the analyst's knowledge of public and nonmaterial nonpublic information about a corporation strengthens the assertion that the analyst reached his or her conclusions solely through appropriate methods rather than through using material nonpublic information.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Mosaic Theory guidance: 'Investment professionals should note, however, that although analysts are free to use mosaic information in their research reports, they should save and document all their research. Evidence of the analyst's knowledge of public and nonmaterial nonpublic information about a corporation strengthens the assertion that the analyst reached his or her conclusions solely through appropriate methods rather than through using material nonpublic information.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Records should be created and kept as the research is done, not reconstructed after an inquiry begins.

questions.stem: *Compensating expert network sources* *most likely*:

options.A: prohibited.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: members and candidates may provide compensation to individuals for their insights without violating this standard.

rationales.A: Compensation OK; MNPI prohibited.
rationales.B: Experts + MNPI.
rationales.C:
Full statement: members and candidates may provide compensation to individuals for their insights without violating this standard; however, members and candidates are ULTIMATELY RESPONSIBLE for ensuring that they are not requesting or acting on confidential information received from external experts, which may violate security regulations and would violate this standard; firms connecting experts with members or candidates often require both parties to sign agreements concerning disclosure of material nonpublic information; even with protections from such compliance practices, if an expert provides MNPI, members and candidates are PROHIBITED from taking investment actions using this information until the information becomes publicly known to the market.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance on Using Industry Experts: 'Members and candidates may provide compensation to individuals for their insights without violating this standard. However, members and candidates are ultimately responsible for ensuring that they are not requesting or acting on confidential information received from external experts, which may violate security regulations and would violate this standard. Firms connecting experts with members or candidates often require both parties to sign agreements concerning the disclosure of material nonpublic information. Even with the protections from such compliance practices, if an expert provides material nonpublic information, members and candidates are prohibited from taking investment actions using this information until the information becomes publicly known to the market.'

questions.stem: *Trading beyond individual securities* under II(A) *most likely*:

options.A: prohibition on using this information goes BEYOND the direct buying and selling of individual securities or bonds.
options.B: only stocks/bonds.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: prohibition on using this information goes BEYOND the direct buying and selling of individual securities or bonds; members and candidates must NOT use material nonpublic information to influence their investment actions related to DERIVATIVES, MUTUAL FUNDS, or other alternative investments; any trading based on material nonpublic information constitutes a violation of Standard II(A).

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance: 'The prohibition on using this information goes beyond the direct buying and selling of individual securities or bonds. Members and candidates must not use material nonpublic information to influence their investment actions related to derivatives, mutual funds, or other alternative investments. Any trading based on material nonpublic information constitutes a violation of Standard II(A).'
rationales.B: All derivatives/funds/alternatives.
rationales.C: Broad prohibition.

questions.stem: *Material information — categories* per curriculum *most likely* includes:

options.A: narrow list.
options.B: material information may include, but is not limited to, information on: earnings.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Broad materiality categories.
rationales.B:
Full statement: material information may include, but is not limited to, information on: earnings; mergers, acquisitions, tender offers, or joint ventures; changes in assets or asset quality; innovative products, processes, or discoveries; new licenses, patents, registered trademarks, or regulatory approval/rejection of product; developments regarding customers or suppliers (e.g., acquisition or loss of a contract); changes in management; change in auditor notification or fact that issuer may no longer rely on auditor's report or qualified opinion; events regarding issuer's securities (defaults on senior securities, calls for redemption, repurchase plans, stock splits, dividend changes, changes to rights of security holders, public/private sales of additional securities); bankruptcies; significant legal disputes; government reports of economic trends (employment, housing starts, currency information); orders for large trades before executed; new or changing equity or debt ratings issued by third party.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance: '...material information may include, but is not limited to, information on the following: earnings; mergers, acquisitions, tender offers, or joint ventures; changes in assets or asset quality; innovative products, processes, or discoveries...; new licenses, patents, registered trademarks, or regulatory approval/rejection of a product; developments regarding customers or suppliers (e.g., the acquisition or loss of a contract); changes in management; change in auditor notification or the fact that the issuer may no longer rely on an auditor's report or qualified opinion; events regarding the issuer's securities...; bankruptcies; significant legal disputes; government reports of economic trends...; orders for large trades before they are executed; and new or changing equity or debt ratings issued by a third party (e.g., sell-side recommendations and credit ratings).'
rationales.C: Categories.

questions.stem: *Materiality — definition* *most likely*:

options.A: Information is material only if its disclosure would move the security's price by a defined regulatory threshold.
options.B: Information is material only when it originates from the company's senior management.
options.C: information is 'material' if its disclosure is likely to have an impact on the price of a security OR if reasonable investors would want to know the information before making an investment decision.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Materiality turns on likely price impact OR on what reasonable investors would want to know; no numeric threshold applies.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Materiality is about content and impact, not the seniority of the source.
rationales.C:
Full statement: information is 'material' if its disclosure is likely to have an impact on the price of a security OR if reasonable investors would want to know the information before making an investment decision; in other words, information is material if it would significantly alter the total mix of information currently available about a security in such a way that the price of the security would be affected.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance: 'Information is "material" if its disclosure is likely to have an impact on the price of a security or if reasonable investors would want to know the information before making an investment decision. In other words, information is material if it would significantly alter the total mix of information currently available about a security in such a way that the price of the security would be affected.'

questions.stem: *Materiality — specificity, difference, nature, reliability* *most likely*:

options.A: the specificity of the information, extent of its difference from public information, its nature, and its reliability are key factors in determining whether a particular piece of information fits the definition of material.
options.B: Materiality is a binary legal determination that does not depend on the information's specificity or nature.
options.C: Only the extent of difference from public information matters; specificity and reliability are irrelevant.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance: 'The specificity of the information, the extent of its difference from public information, its nature, and its reliability are key factors in determining whether a particular piece of information fits the definition of material.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Specificity, difference from public information, nature, and reliability are the key assessment factors.
rationales.C: Incorrect. All four factors — including specificity and reliability — bear on the determination.

questions.stem: *Trading or inducing others erodes market confidence* *most likely*:

options.A: Trading on material nonpublic information improves market efficiency by moving prices toward fair value faster.
options.B: trading or inducing others to trade on material nonpublic information erodes confidence in capital markets, institutions, and investment professionals by supporting the idea that those with inside information and special access can take unfair advantage of the general investing public.
options.C: The harm from insider trading is confined to the counterparty on the specific trade.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Whatever its price effect, MNPI trading erodes confidence in markets, institutions, and professionals by rewarding informational advantage over skill.
rationales.B:
Full statement: trading or inducing others to trade on material nonpublic information erodes confidence in capital markets, institutions, and investment professionals by supporting the idea that those with inside information and special access can take unfair advantage of the general investing public; although trading on inside information may lead to short-term profits, in the long run, individuals and the profession as a whole suffer from such trading; these actions have caused and will continue to cause investors to avoid capital markets because markets are perceived to be 'rigged' in favor of the knowledgeable insider; when investing public avoids capital markets, markets and capital allocation become less efficient and less supportive of strong and vibrant economies.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance: 'Trading or inducing others to trade on material nonpublic information erodes confidence in capital markets, institutions, and investment professionals by supporting the idea that those with inside information and special access can take unfair advantage of the general investing public. Although trading on inside information may lead to short-term profits, in the long run, individuals and the profession as a whole suffer from such trading. These actions have caused and will continue to cause investors to avoid capital markets because the markets are perceived to be "rigged" in favor of the knowledgeable insider.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The damage is systemic — investor confidence in the fairness of capital markets — not merely bilateral.

questions.stem: *Company-provided guidance on public financials* *most likely*:

options.A: Guidance a company gives an analyst about previously released financials is public by definition.
options.B: Interpretation assistance from investor relations can never rise to the level of material information.
options.C: analysts should also be alert to the possibility that they are selectively receiving material nonpublic information when a company provides them with GUIDANCE or INTERPRETATION of such publicly available information as financial statements or regulatory filings.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Guidance or interpretation provided selectively — even about public financials — can itself be selectively received MNPI.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Interpretive guidance can be material when it changes the meaning of the public record for the recipient.
rationales.C:
Full statement: analysts should also be alert to the possibility that they are selectively receiving material nonpublic information when a company provides them with GUIDANCE or INTERPRETATION of such publicly available information as financial statements or regulatory filings; even when underlying information is public, additional interpretation from company insider may itself constitute MNPI.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance: 'Analysts should also be alert to the possibility that they are selectively receiving material nonpublic information when a company provides them with guidance or interpretation of such publicly available information as financial statements or regulatory filings.'

questions.stem: *Example 1 FIS*: principal owner agrees to promote two microcaps for stock and cash compensation; disseminates emails, websites, online newsletter — all recommending investment; publications purportedly independent analyses containing inaccurate and highly promotional/speculative statements. *Most likely*:

options.A: principal owner of FIS violated Standard II(B) by using inaccurate reporting and misleading information under the guise of independent analysis to artificially increase the stock price of the companies.
options.B: There is no violation because the publications carried disclaimers and readers could verify the claims themselves.
options.C: The conduct implicates only Standard I(C) Misrepresentation, not the market-integrity standards.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Example 1 Comment: 'The principal owner of FIS violated Standard II(B) by using inaccurate reporting and misleading information under the guise of independent analysis to artificially increase the stock price of the companies.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Misleading promotion dressed as independent analysis to inflate prices is manipulation; disclaimers do not cure the deception.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Using inaccurate reporting under the guise of independence to pump the stocks is information-based manipulation under II(B).

questions.stem: *Example 2 Gray/Fame Pharmaceuticals*: divides holdings into multiple accounts in names of family/friends/religious institution; creates rumor campaign on blogs/social media; buys and sells stock through multiple brokers, selling slightly larger positions than he bought on tactical schedule to raise volume and price. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because Gray traded accounts belonging to family, friends, and a religious institution.
options.B: Gray violated Standard II(B) by fraudulently creating appearance that there was greater investor interest in the stock through the online rumors.
options.C: Only the online rumor campaign violated the standard; the inter-account trading was legitimate liquidity provision.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Using nominee accounts to fabricate the appearance of investor interest is manipulation regardless of whose names the accounts carry.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Gray violated Standard II(B) by fraudulently creating appearance that there was greater investor interest in the stock through the online rumors; additionally, through his trading strategy, he created appearance that there was greater liquidity in the stock than actually existed; he manipulated the price through both misinformation and trading practices.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'Gray violated Standard II(B) by fraudulently creating the appearance that there was greater investor interest in the stock through the online rumors. Additionally, through his trading strategy, he created the appearance that there was greater liquidity in the stock than actually existed. He manipulated the price through both misinformation and trading practices.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The circular trading created artificial volume — transaction-based manipulation alongside the information-based rumors.

questions.stem: *Example 3 Murphy/Wirewolf*: alerts sales force about incoming Wirewolf report during quarter-end quiet period; conclusions based on speculation not fact; times release specifically to sensationalize negative aspects for distinct advantage of Divisadero's short-holding hedge fund clients. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because sales-force alerts about upcoming research are routine industry practice.
options.B: Murphy's only failing is the report's speculative basis, a diligence issue under Standard V(A).
options.C: Murphy violated Standard II(B) by aiming to create artificial price volatility designed to have a material impact on the price of an issuer's stock.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Timing a speculation-based release to sensationalize and move the stock aims at artificial volatility — II(B).
rationales.B: Incorrect. Weak basis is part of it, but the manipulative intent to create artificial price impact is the II(B) violation.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Example 3 Comment: 'Murphy violated Standard II(B) by aiming to create artificial price volatility designed to have a material impact on the price of an issuer's stock.'

questions.stem: *Example 4 Sekar/Digital Design*: manages two funds; DD holdings excessive but stock has low liquidity; decides to trade larger portions of DD back and forth between his two funds to slowly increase price so market participants get interested. *Most likely*:

options.A: Sekar's plan would be beneficial for his funds' participants but is based on artificial distortion of both trading volume and the price of the DD stock and thus constitutes a violation of Standard II(B).
options.B: There is no violation because the plan would benefit the funds' participants by improving reported liquidity.
options.C: Trading between two funds under common management is internal rebalancing outside the standard's scope.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Example 4 Comment: 'Sekar's plan would be beneficial for his funds' participants but is based on artificial distortion of both trading volume and the price of the DD stock and thus constitutes a violation of Standard II(B).'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Beneficial intent does not excuse artificially distorting trading volume and price — the plan is manipulation.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Wash-style trades between commonly managed funds still distort the market's picture of volume and price.

questions.stem: *Example 5 Gordon/Hygene*: exaggerates earnings projections ($0.15-$2.15 above guidance) to force company to update guidance and fuel quick gain for momentum-style clients. *Most likely*:

options.A: The projections are acceptable because analysts may publish any earnings estimate they genuinely believe possible.
options.B: by exaggerating her earnings projections in order to try to fuel a quick gain in Hygene's stock price, Gordon is in violation of Standard II(B).
options.C: Gordon's conduct violates Standard II(A) because her projections were based on nonpublic information.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Deliberately exaggerated projections designed to force guidance updates and fuel a quick gain are manipulative — belief in an outside possibility does not cure intent.
rationales.B:
Full statement: by exaggerating her earnings projections in order to try to fuel a quick gain in Hygene's stock price, Gordon is in violation of Standard II(B); however, it would have been acceptable for Gordon to write a report that (1) framed her earnings projection in a RANGE of possible outcomes and (2) outlined CLEARLY the assumptions used in her Hygene models that took into consideration findings from her trip through Latin America.

Correct. Per Example 5 Comment: 'By exaggerating her earnings projections in order to try to fuel a quick gain in Hygene's stock price, Gordon is in violation of Standard II(B). However, it would have been acceptable for Gordon to write a report that (1) framed her earnings projection in a range of possible outcomes and (2) outlined clearly the assumptions used in her Hygene models that took into consideration the findings from her trip through Latin America.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. No MNPI is involved; the violation is information-based manipulation under II(B).

questions.stem: *Example 6 Weinberg (pump and dump)*: logs on to several investor chatrooms to start rumors that Moosehead & Belfast Railroad is about to expand rail network in anticipation of large lumber contract. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because chatroom participants understand that posts are speculation.
options.B: Weinberg violated Standard II(A) by trading while in possession of material nonpublic information.
options.C: Weinberg violated Standard II(B) by disseminating false information about Moosehead & Belfast with the intent to mislead market participants.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Disseminating false expansion rumors with intent to mislead the market is manipulation; audience skepticism is no defense.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The rumors were fabricated, not inside information; the violation is II(B) market manipulation.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Example 6 Comment: 'Weinberg violated Standard II(B) by disseminating false information about Moosehead & Belfast with the intent to mislead market participants.'

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 5 Pietro/Local Bank*: bank looking for merger partner; potential buyers waiting for next period's worse data. Actions most likely violation *most likely*:

options.A: place buy order for 2,000 shares (four times average weekly volume) through Vogt for his personal account.
options.B: Instruct press release announcing bank retained Vogt.
options.C: Instruct release reaffirming firm's prior earnings guidance.

rationales.A:
Full statement: place buy order for 2,000 shares (four times average weekly volume) through Vogt for his personal account; through placing a personal purchase order that is significantly greater than average volume, Pietro is violating Standard II(B) Market Manipulation — he is attempting to manipulate an increase in the share price and thus bring a buyer to the negotiating table; the news of possible merger and confirmation of firm's earnings guidance may also have positive effects but Pietro's actions in instructing release of information does not represent violation through market manipulation; announcements are common and practical to keep investors informed.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 5 Solution: 'Through placing a personal purchase order that is significantly greater than the average volume, Pietro is violating Standard II(B) Market Manipulation. He is attempting to manipulate an increase in the share price and thus bring a buyer to the negotiating table. The news of a possible merger and confirmation of the firm's earnings guidance may also have positive effects on the price of Local Bank, but Pietro's actions in instructing the release of the information does not represent a violation through market manipulation. Announcements of this nature are common and practical to keep investors informed.'
rationales.B: Large personal buy = manipulation.
rationales.C: Volume manipulation.

questions.stem: *Information-based manipulation* under II(B) *most likely*:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: information-based manipulation includes, but is not limited to, spreading false rumors to induce trading by others.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Pump and dump.
rationales.B:
Full statement: information-based manipulation includes, but is not limited to, spreading false rumors to induce trading by others; members and candidates must refrain from 'pumping up' the price of an investment by issuing misleading positive information or overly optimistic projections of a security's worth only to later 'dump' the investment (i.e., sell it) once the price, fueled by the misleading information's effect on other market participants, reaches an artificially high level.

Correct. Per Standard II(B) Guidance on Information-Based Manipulation: 'Information-based manipulation includes, but is not limited to, spreading false rumors to induce trading by others. For example, members and candidates must refrain from "pumping up" the price of an investment by issuing misleading positive information or overly optimistic projections of a security's worth only to later "dump" the investment (i.e., sell it) once the price, fueled by the misleading information's effect on other market participants, reaches an artificially high level.'
rationales.C: Info-based.

questions.stem: *Transaction-based manipulation* under II(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Transaction-based manipulation requires proof that prices actually moved as a result of the trades.
options.B: Transaction-based manipulation covers only trades executed through undisclosed nominee accounts.
options.C: transaction-based manipulation involves instances where member/candidate knows or should know that his or her actions could distort the price of a security.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Knowing — or having reason to know — that one's actions COULD distort prices suffices; realized price impact is not the test.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The category includes any volume- or price-distorting transactions, such as wash trades and securing dominant positions, however executed.
rationales.C:
Full statement: transaction-based manipulation involves instances where member/candidate knows or should know that his or her actions could distort the price of a security; this type of manipulation includes, but is not limited to: (1) transactions that artificially affect prices or volume to give impression of activity or price movement in a financial instrument, which represent diversion from expectations of fair and efficient market; (2) securing a controlling, dominant position in a financial instrument to exploit and manipulate the price of a related derivative and/or the underlying asset.

Correct. Per Standard II(B) Guidance on Transaction-Based Manipulation: 'Transaction-based manipulation involves instances where a member or candidate knows or should know that his or her actions could distort the price of a security. This type of manipulation includes, but is not limited to, the following: Transactions that artificially affect prices or volume to give the impression of activity or price movement in a financial instrument, which represent a diversion from the expectations of a fair and efficient market; Securing a controlling, dominant position in a financial instrument to exploit and manipulate the price of a related derivative and/or the underlying asset.'

questions.stem: *Legitimate strategies exempt* under II(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Standard II(B) is NOT intended to preclude transactions undertaken on legitimate trading strategies based on perceived market inefficiencies.
options.B: Any trading strategy that produces unusually high volume presumptively violates Standard II(B).
options.C: Exploiting a perceived market inefficiency is manipulation because it profits from other participants' mistakes.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Standard II(B) is NOT intended to preclude transactions undertaken on legitimate trading strategies based on perceived market inefficiencies; legitimate trading activity may APPEAR abusive, especially where the market is illiquid or volatile; the INTENT of the action is critical to determining whether it is a violation of this standard.

Correct. Per Standard II(B) Guidance: 'Standard II(B) is not intended to preclude transactions undertaken on legitimate trading strategies based on perceived market inefficiencies. Legitimate trading activity may appear abusive, especially where the market is illiquid or volatile. The intent of the action is critical to determining whether it is a violation of this standard.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Volume alone is not the test; the INTENT to mislead market participants is the critical element.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Legitimate strategies based on perceived inefficiencies are expressly outside the standard's aim.

questions.stem: *Market manipulation indicators* under II(B) *most likely* include:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: orders entered for an unusually short time period.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Signal list.
rationales.B:
Full statement: orders entered for an unusually short time period; orders above market price; orders resulting in no change in beneficial ownership; orders that intend to change the bid or offer prices and are canceled before they are executed; orders on both sides of the trade using different platforms; coordinated action by two or more traders in a market with a small number of participants.

Correct. Per Standard II(B) Guidance: 'The following is a nonexhaustive list of indicators that may signal market manipulation: Orders entered for an unusually short time period; Orders above market price; Orders resulting in no change in beneficial ownership; Orders that intend to change the bid or offer prices and are canceled before they are executed; Orders on both sides of the trade using different platforms; Coordinated action by two or more traders in a market with a small number of participants.'
rationales.C: Indicators.

questions.stem: *Damage from market manipulation* *most likely*:

options.A: Market manipulation is a victimless offense because prices eventually revert to fundamental value.
options.B: Manipulation harms only the unsophisticated retail investors who trade against the manipulator.
options.C: market manipulation damages the interests of all investors by disrupting the smooth functioning of financial markets and lowering investor confidence.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Manipulation disrupts market functioning and lowers investor confidence even if prices later revert.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The damage extends to ALL investors through impaired market integrity, not just direct counterparties.
rationales.C:
Full statement: market manipulation damages the interests of all investors by disrupting the smooth functioning of financial markets and lowering investor confidence; market manipulation may lead to a lack of trust in fairness of the capital markets, resulting in higher risk premiums and reduced investor participation; a reduction in efficiency of a local capital market may negatively affect growth and economic health of the country and may also influence operations of the globally interconnected capital markets.

Correct. Per Standard II(B) Guidance: 'Market manipulation damages the interests of all investors by disrupting the smooth functioning of financial markets and lowering investor confidence. Market manipulation may lead to a lack of trust in the fairness of the capital markets, resulting in higher risk premiums and reduced investor participation. A reduction in the efficiency of a local capital market may negatively affect the growth and economic health of the country and may also influence the operations of the globally interconnected capital markets.'

questions.stem: *Standard II(A) requirement* *most likely*:

options.A: Members and Candidates who possess material nonpublic information that could affect the value of an investment must not act or cause others to act on the information.
options.B: Members may act on material nonpublic information provided they first share it equally with all their clients.
options.C: The prohibition lapses once the member reasonably believes someone else has already traded on the information.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Standard II(A): 'Members and Candidates who possess material nonpublic information that could affect the value of an investment must not act or cause others to act on the information.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Sharing MNPI with clients is CAUSING OTHERS TO ACT — a second violation, not a cure.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Others' misuse does not license the member's own trading; the prohibition holds until public dissemination.

questions.stem: *Standard II(B) requirement* *most likely*:

options.A: Price-distorting practices are permitted if the member discloses the strategy to the exchange in advance.
options.B: Members and Candidates must not engage in practices that distort prices or artificially inflate trading volume with the intent to mislead market participants.
options.C: Standard II(B) prohibits only volume inflation, not practices that distort prices.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Disclosure does not authorize practices intended to mislead market participants.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard II(B): 'Members and Candidates must not engage in practices that distort prices or artificially inflate trading volume with the intent to mislead market participants.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Both price distortion and artificial volume inflation with intent to mislead are prohibited.

questions.stem: *Standard II(A) intent — is loss defense?* *most likely*:

options.A: A member who loses money on an MNPI-based trade has not violated the standard, since no advantage was gained.
options.B: Losses convert an MNPI violation into a lesser record-keeping infraction.
options.C: the fact that a member/candidate lost money on a trade based on material nonpublic information does NOT absolve the member/candidate for trading on MNPI.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The violation is acting on MNPI; the trade's outcome is irrelevant.
rationales.B: Incorrect. There is no downgrade mechanism — the prohibition is on the act, not the profit.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the fact that a member/candidate lost money on a trade based on material nonpublic information does NOT absolve the member/candidate for trading on MNPI; it is the member's or candidate's responsibility to make sure, before executing investment actions, that comments about earnings are not material nonpublic information; per Example 8 (Kellogg): even though he lost money, he violated Standard II(A).

Correct. Per Example 8 Comment (Kellogg): 'The fact that he lost money on the purchase does not absolve Kellogg for trading on material nonpublic information. It is the member's or candidate's responsibility to make sure, before executing investment actions, that comments about earnings are not material nonpublic information.'

drills.los_text: recommend practices and procedures designed to prevent violations of the Code and Standards
drills.reading_name: Guidance for Standard II: Integrity of Capital Markets

questions.stem: *Achieve Public Dissemination* under II(A) *most likely*:

options.A: if member/candidate obtains material nonpublic information, should make reasonable efforts to achieve public dissemination of the information.
options.B: A member who obtains material nonpublic information should destroy it and tell no one.
options.C: The member should quietly wait for the information to become stale before resuming coverage.

rationales.A:
Full statement: if member/candidate obtains material nonpublic information, should make reasonable efforts to achieve public dissemination of the information; these efforts usually entail encouraging the issuing company to make the information public; if public dissemination is not possible, member/candidate must communicate the information only to the designated supervisory and compliance personnel within his/her firm and must not take investment action or alter current investment recommendations based on the information; moreover, must not knowingly engage in any conduct that may induce company insiders to privately disclose material nonpublic information.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Compliance Practices — Achieve Public Dissemination: 'If a member or candidate obtains material nonpublic information, the member or candidate should make reasonable efforts to achieve public dissemination of the information. These efforts usually entail encouraging the issuing company to make the information public. If public dissemination is not possible, the member or candidate must communicate the information only to the designated supervisory and compliance personnel within the member's or candidate's firm and must not take investment action or alter current investment recommendations based on the information. Moreover, members and candidates must not knowingly engage in any conduct that may induce company insiders to privately disclose material nonpublic information.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The recommended course is making reasonable efforts to ACHIEVE PUBLIC DISSEMINATION — typically by encouraging the company to release it.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Passive waiting is not the guidance; dissemination efforts (and compliance notification) are.

questions.stem: *Adopt Compliance Procedures* under II(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Employer compliance procedures on MNPI are advisory; members may deviate when their judgment differs.
options.B: members and candidates must comply with all compliance procedures adopted by their employer to prevent misuse of material nonpublic information.
options.C: Compliance procedures bind only supervisors, not the analysts and managers they oversee.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Members MUST comply with employer procedures adopted to prevent MNPI misuse.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates must comply with all compliance procedures adopted by their employer to prevent misuse of material nonpublic information; such procedures might address such areas as: review of employee and proprietary trading; review of investment recommendations; supervision of interdepartmental communications in multiservice firms; informing compliance personnel of suspected inappropriate use of material nonpublic information.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Compliance Practices — Adopt Compliance Procedures: 'Members and candidates must comply with all compliance procedures adopted by their employer to prevent the misuse of material nonpublic information. Such procedures might address such areas as the review of employee and proprietary trading, the review of investment recommendations, the supervision of interdepartmental communications in multiservice firms, and informing compliance personnel of suspected inappropriate use of material nonpublic information.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The compliance duty applies to every member and candidate at the firm.

questions.stem: *Issue Press Releases* practice under II(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Members should discourage press releases before analyst calls so the call retains news value.
options.B: Meeting scripts are the company's internal matter and not something analysts should encourage.
options.C: members and candidates should encourage companies they follow to issue press releases prior to analyst meetings and conference calls and to script those meetings and calls to decrease the chance that further information will be disclosed.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Members should ENCOURAGE pre-call press releases so the information is public before the meeting.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Encouraging scripted meetings is part of the recommended practice to prevent selective disclosure.
rationales.C:
Full statement: members and candidates should encourage companies they follow to issue press releases prior to analyst meetings and conference calls and to script those meetings and calls to decrease the chance that further information will be disclosed; if material nonpublic information is disclosed for the first time in an analyst meeting or call, members and candidates should encourage the company to promptly issue a press release or otherwise make the information publicly available.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Compliance Practices — Issue Press Releases: 'Members and candidates should encourage companies they follow to issue press releases prior to analyst meetings and conference calls and to script those meetings and calls to decrease the chance that further information will be disclosed. If material nonpublic information is disclosed for the first time in an analyst meeting or call, members and candidates should encourage the company to promptly issue a press release or otherwise make the information publicly available.'
