questions.stem: *Practice Problem 2 investment banking / brokerage best policy* *most likely*:

options.A: establish physical and informational barriers within firm to prevent exchange of information between the investment banking and brokerage operations.
options.B: Permanent prohibition of buy/sell recs of investment banking clients.
options.C: Monitor exchange of information between IB and brokerage.

rationales.A:
Full statement: establish physical and informational barriers within firm to prevent exchange of information between the investment banking and brokerage operations; the best policy to prevent violation of Standard II(A) is the establishment of firewalls in a firm to prevent exchange of insider information; physical and informational barrier of a firewall between investment banking department and brokerage operation prevents the investment banking department from providing information to analysts on the brokerage side who may be writing recommendations on a company stock; prohibiting recommendations is an alternative — but permanent prohibition is not the best answer because once an offering is complete and MNPI becomes public, resuming publishing is not a violation; monitoring exchanges is not effective because NO exchange of information should be occurring between the departments.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 2 Solution: 'The best policy to prevent violation of Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information is the establishment of firewalls in a firm to prevent exchange of insider information. The physical and informational barrier of a firewall between the investment banking department and the brokerage operation prevents the investment banking department from providing information to analysts on the brokerage side who may be writing recommendations on a company stock... Once an offering is complete and the material nonpublic information obtained by the investment banking department becomes public, resuming publishing recommendations on the stock is not a violation of the Code and Standards... C is incorrect because no exchange of information should be occurring between the investment banking department and the brokerage operation, so monitoring of such exchanges is not an effective compliance procedure for preventing the use of material nonpublic information.'
rationales.B: Firewalls are best.
rationales.C: Firewalls best.

questions.stem: *Firewall elements* under II(A) *most likely*:

options.A: A firewall consists solely of physical separation between departments on different floors.
options.B: elements of firewall system include, but not limited to: substantial control of relevant interdepartmental communications, preferably through clearance area within firm in either compliance or legal department.
options.C: Interdepartmental communication may flow freely provided it is logged after the fact.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Physical separation is one element among several — control of communications through a clearance area, review of employee trading, documentation, and heightened proprietary-trading scrutiny.
rationales.B:
Full statement: elements of firewall system include, but not limited to: substantial control of relevant interdepartmental communications, preferably through clearance area within firm in either compliance or legal department; review of employee trading through maintenance of 'watch,' 'restricted,' and 'rumor' lists; documentation of procedures designed to limit flow of information between departments and of actions taken to enforce those procedures; heightened review or restriction of proprietary trading while firm is in possession of material nonpublic information.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Compliance Practices — Comply with Firewall Restrictions and Procedures: 'Elements of such a system include, but are not limited to, the following: substantial control of relevant interdepartmental communications, preferably through a clearance area within the firm in either the compliance or legal department; review of employee trading through the maintenance of "watch," "restricted," and "rumor" lists; documentation of the procedures designed to limit the flow of information between departments and of the actions taken to enforce those procedures; and heightened review or restriction of proprietary trading while a firm is in possession of material nonpublic information.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Relevant communications are substantially CONTROLLED (preferably via a compliance clearance area), not free-flowing with retrospective logs.

questions.stem: *No overlap of personnel* under firewalls *most likely*:

options.A: Staff may rotate freely between investment banking and research so long as projects do not overlap in time.
options.B: Overlap restrictions apply to junior staff only; senior officers may span both sides of the wall.
options.C: there should be NO overlap of personnel between investment banking and corporate finance areas of a brokerage firm and the sales and research departments or between a bank's commercial lending department and its trust and research departments.

rationales.A: Incorrect. There should be NO overlap of personnel between the banking/corporate-finance side and sales/research.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The no-overlap principle is not seniority-based; spanning both sides defeats the barrier.
rationales.C:
Full statement: there should be NO overlap of personnel between investment banking and corporate finance areas of a brokerage firm and the sales and research departments or between a bank's commercial lending department and its trust and research departments; for a firewall to be effective in a multiservice firm, an employee should be on ONLY ONE side of the firewall at any time.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance: 'There should be no overlap of personnel between the investment banking and corporate finance areas of a brokerage firm and the sales and research departments or between a bank's commercial lending department and its trust and research departments. For a firewall to be effective in a multiservice firm, an employee should be on only one side of the firewall at any time.'

questions.stem: *Analyst crossing wall* *most likely*:

options.A: analyst who follows a particular company may provide limited assistance to investment bankers under carefully controlled circumstances when firm's investment banking department is involved in a deal with the company.
options.B: An analyst who assists investment bankers on a deal faces no restrictions once the project ends.
options.C: Research analysts may never provide any assistance to the investment banking department.

rationales.A:
Full statement: analyst who follows a particular company may provide limited assistance to investment bankers under carefully controlled circumstances when firm's investment banking department is involved in a deal with the company; that analyst must then be treated as though he/she were an investment banker; must remain on investment banking side of the wall until any information he/she learns is publicly disclosed; in short, analyst cannot use any information learned in the course of the project for research purposes and cannot share that information with colleagues in research department.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance: 'An analyst who follows a particular company may provide limited assistance to the investment bankers under carefully controlled circumstances when the firm's investment banking department is involved in a deal with the company. That analyst must then be treated as though he or she were an investment banker; the analyst must remain on the investment banking side of the wall until any information he or she learns is publicly disclosed. In short, the analyst cannot use any information learned in the course of the project for research purposes and cannot share that information with colleagues in the research department.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The analyst is treated as a banking-side participant — restricted until the information becomes public, not merely until the project ends.
rationales.C: Incorrect. LIMITED assistance under carefully controlled, compliance-monitored circumstances is permitted.

questions.stem: *Reporting system for firewall procedures* *most likely*:

options.A: The objective of a firewall is to eliminate all communication between departments permanently.
options.B: primary objective of an effective firewall procedure is to establish a reporting system in which authorized people review and approve communications between departments.
options.C: Reviews of cross-departmental communication may be performed by the requesting employees themselves.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The objective is a REPORTING SYSTEM in which authorized personnel review and approve necessary cross-wall communications, not a total ban.
rationales.B:
Full statement: primary objective of an effective firewall procedure is to establish a reporting system in which authorized people review and approve communications between departments; if employee behind firewall believes he/she needs to share confidential information with someone on the other side, employee should consult designated compliance officer to determine whether sharing is necessary and how much information should be shared; if sharing is necessary, compliance officer should coordinate the process of 'LOOKING OVER THE WALL' so that necessary information will be shared and integrity of procedure will be maintained.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance: 'A primary objective of an effective firewall procedure is to establish a reporting system in which authorized people review and approve communications between departments. If an employee behind a firewall believes that he or she needs to share confidential information with someone on the other side of the firewall, the employee should consult a designated compliance officer to determine whether sharing the information is necessary and how much information should be shared. If the sharing is necessary, the compliance officer should coordinate the process of "looking over the wall" so that the necessary information will be shared and the integrity of the procedure will be maintained.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Review and approval sit with authorized compliance personnel, not the interested employees.

questions.stem: *Comply with Personal Trading Limitations* under II(A) *most likely*:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: members and candidates must comply with their employers' restrictions or prohibitions on personal trading by employees.

rationales.A: Personal trading limits.
rationales.B: Personal trading.
rationales.C:
Full statement: members and candidates must comply with their employers' restrictions or prohibitions on personal trading by employees; such practices may include firm monitoring of personal trading, mandating periodic reports of personal transactions, and implementing a restricted list and watch list of companies.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Compliance Practices — Comply with Personal Trading Limitations: 'Members and candidates must comply with their employers' restrictions or prohibitions on personal trading by employees. Such practices may include firm monitoring of personal trading, mandating periodic reports of personal transactions, and implementing a restricted list and watch list of companies.'

questions.stem: *Restricted list use* under II(A) *most likely*:

options.A: guidance recommends adding securities to firm's restricted list when firm has or may have material nonpublic information.
options.B: Restricted lists are discouraged under II(A) because they signal to the market that the firm holds MNPI.
options.C: Securities should be added to the restricted list only after an insider-trading inquiry has begun.

rationales.A:
Full statement: guidance recommends adding securities to firm's restricted list when firm has or may have material nonpublic information; by adding these securities to this list, member/candidate would uphold Standard II(A); restricted list is one of the tools that prevents both trading and issuance of recommendations while firm holds MNPI.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 3 Solution: 'The guidance for Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information recommends adding securities to the firm's restricted list when the firm has or may have material nonpublic information. By adding these securities to this list, Andrews would uphold this standard.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Adding securities to the restricted list when the firm has or may have MNPI is the recommended practice.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The list is preventive — used when MNPI may exist, not after an inquiry starts.

questions.stem: *Firewalls in multiservice firms* *most likely*:

options.A: Firewalls have fallen out of use because electronic communication makes information barriers unenforceable.
options.B: an information barrier commonly referred to as 'firewall' is the most widely used approach for preventing the communication of material nonpublic information within firms.
options.C: A firewall's purpose is to block communication between the firm and its regulators.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The information barrier ('firewall') remains the most widely used approach to containing MNPI within multiservice firms.
rationales.B:
Full statement: an information barrier commonly referred to as 'firewall' is the most widely used approach for preventing the communication of material nonpublic information within firms; it restricts the flow of confidential information to those who need to know the information to perform their jobs effectively.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Compliance Practices: 'An information barrier commonly referred to as a "firewall" is the most widely used approach for preventing the communication of material nonpublic information within firms. It restricts the flow of confidential information to those who need to know the information to perform their jobs effectively.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Firewalls restrict interdepartmental flow of MNPI inside the firm — regulator communication is unaffected.

questions.stem: *Achieve public dissemination if not possible: internal actions* *most likely*:

options.A: If public dissemination is not possible, the member may still trade after documenting the attempt.
options.B: The member should circulate the information to all portfolio managers so no client is disadvantaged.
options.C: if public dissemination is not possible, member/candidate must communicate the information ONLY to the designated supervisory and compliance personnel within his/her firm and must NOT take investment action or alter current investment recommendations based on the information.

rationales.A: Incorrect. When dissemination cannot be achieved, the member must not act — documentation does not restore trading freedom.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The information goes ONLY to designated supervisory and compliance personnel; broad circulation multiplies the violation.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Standard II(A) Compliance Practices: 'If public dissemination is not possible, the member or candidate must communicate the information only to the designated supervisory and compliance personnel within the member's or candidate's firm and must not take investment action or alter current investment recommendations based on the information.'

questions.stem: *Never induce insiders to privately disclose* *most likely*:

options.A: members and candidates must NOT knowingly engage in any conduct that may induce company insiders to privately disclose material nonpublic information.
options.B: Members may encourage insiders to share private details so long as no payment changes hands.
options.C: The prohibition applies only to direct requests, not to conduct designed to elicit disclosure.

rationales.A:
Full statement: members and candidates must NOT knowingly engage in any conduct that may induce company insiders to privately disclose material nonpublic information; this is part of the achieve public dissemination compliance practice.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Compliance Practices — Achieve Public Dissemination: 'Moreover, members and candidates must not knowingly engage in any conduct that may induce company insiders to privately disclose material nonpublic information.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Knowingly inducing private disclosure of MNPI is prohibited, compensated or not.
rationales.C: Incorrect. ANY conduct that may induce insiders to disclose privately is covered, not just explicit requests.

questions.stem: *Handling social media groups with membership limitations* *most likely*:

options.A: Information in membership-limited groups may be treated as public because members could repost it.
options.B: members and candidates participating in groups with membership limitations should VERIFY that material information obtained from these sources can also be accessed from a source that would be considered available to the public BEFORE using that information.
options.C: Members should simply avoid joining any limited-membership online group.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The member must VERIFY that material information from such groups is also publicly accessible before using it.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance on Social Media: 'Members and candidates participating in groups with membership limitations should verify that material information obtained from these sources can also be accessed from a source that would be considered available to the public before using that information.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Participation is acceptable; the duty is verification of public availability, not abstention.

questions.stem: *Expert-network agreements not sufficient* *most likely*:

options.A: A signed expert-network agreement stating no MNPI will be conveyed fully protects the receiving analyst.
options.B: Responsibility for screening expert-network calls rests with the network operator, not the analyst.
options.C: Watson (Example 11) illustrates: analyst cannot simply rely on the agreements signed by individuals who participate in expert networks that state that he has not received information that would prohibit his trading activity.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The analyst cannot simply rely on the agreements; each piece of information received must be independently assessed for materiality.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The analyst who receives and would use the information bears the assessment duty.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Watson (Example 11) illustrates: analyst cannot simply rely on the agreements signed by individuals who participate in expert networks that state that he has not received information that would prohibit his trading activity; must make his own determination whether information he received through these arrangements reaches a materiality threshold that would influence his trading.

Correct. Per Example 11 Comment (Watson): 'Watson cannot simply rely on the agreements signed by individuals who participate in expert networks that state that he has not received information that would prohibit his trading activity. He must make his own determination whether information he received through these arrangements reaches a materiality threshold that would influence his trading.'

questions.stem: *Firm procedures to prevent placing/canceling orders with intent to deceive* under II(B) *most likely*:

options.A: members and candidates must comply with their firm's policies and procedures designed to prevent manipulative trading, which could include procedures designed to prevent placing and canceling orders with the intent to deceive others into buying or selling securities at artificial prices.
options.B: Order placement and cancellation patterns are outside compliance's remit because they involve no executed trades.
options.C: Firm procedures need address only executed manipulative trades, not deceptive order entry.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Standard II(B) Compliance Practices: 'Procedures designed to prevent placing and canceling orders with the intent to deceive others into buying or selling securities at artificial prices.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Placing and canceling orders with intent to deceive (e.g., spoofing) is manipulative conduct the procedures target.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Deceptive order entry is covered even when the orders never execute.

questions.stem: *Real-time monitoring across platforms* under II(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Surveillance obligations are satisfied by end-of-day exception reports run on the firm's primary trading venue alone, since intraday monitoring across every platform would be operationally impractical.
options.B: real-time monitoring or systematic surveillance practices across multiple platforms that are designed to identify such activities as suspicious order entries, potential improper coordination among customers, wash trading, transactions in cross-product securities that manipulate the price of an underlying security, and other types of manipulative trading activity.
options.C: Monitoring order flow for suspicious entries and potential collusion is exclusively a self-regulatory-organization function, so firms should avoid building duplicative internal surveillance capabilities.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The practice described is REAL-TIME, systematic surveillance across MULTIPLE platforms.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard II(B) Compliance Practices: 'Real-time monitoring or systematic surveillance practices across multiple platforms that are designed to identify such activities as suspicious order entries, potential improper coordination among customers, wash trading, transactions in cross-product securities that manipulate the price of an underlying security, and other types of manipulative trading activity.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Firm-level monitoring is a recommended compliance practice complementing regulatory oversight.

questions.stem: *Automated order management systems* under II(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Automated order controls are discouraged because they may block legitimate client orders.
options.B: Pretrade controls are needed only for algorithmic strategies, not manual order flow.
options.C: implementation of an automated order management system and pretrade controls to attempt to identify and prevent manipulative orders.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Automated order management with pretrade controls is a recommended tool for identifying and preventing manipulative orders.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Manipulative orders can be entered manually; controls apply to order flow generally.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Standard II(B) Compliance Practices: 'Implementation of an automated order management system and pretrade controls to attempt to identify and prevent manipulative orders.'

questions.stem: *Controls addressing trading algorithms* under II(B) *most likely*:

options.A: controls that address the use of trading algorithms for manipulative trading practices.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: controls that address the use of trading algorithms for manipulative trading practices; algorithms represent a specific area of concern because their speed and complexity can be leveraged for manipulation without human oversight of individual trades.

Correct. Per Standard II(B) Compliance Practices: 'Controls that address the use of trading algorithms for manipulative trading practices.'
rationales.B: Algo controls.
rationales.C: Algo controls.

questions.stem: *Trade exception reporting* under II(B) *most likely*:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: trade exception reporting.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Exception reporting.
rationales.B:
Full statement: trade exception reporting — a compliance practice designed to identify trades that deviate from expected patterns or trigger established thresholds, allowing compliance and supervisory personnel to review the trades for possible manipulative activity.

Correct. Per Standard II(B) Compliance Practices: 'Trade exception reporting.'
rationales.C: Exception reporting.

questions.stem: *Alternative to permanent prohibition* *most likely*:

options.A: There is no recognized alternative to physical information barriers; every multiservice firm must maintain firewalls between banking and research regardless of size, structure, or business mix.
options.B: The preferred solution is a permanent, irrevocable prohibition on recommending the securities of any company that has ever been an investment banking client of the firm.
options.C: prohibiting recommendations of stock of companies that are clients of investment banking department is an ALTERNATIVE to firewalls, but PERMANENT prohibition would not be best answer because once an offering is complete and MNPI obtained by IB department becomes public, resuming publishing recommendations on the stock is not a violation of Code and Standards.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Prohibiting recommendations of banking clients' securities is a recognized alternative to firewalls.
rationales.B: Incorrect. A PERMANENT prohibition would not be practical or fair to research clients — the restriction is temporary, tied to the engagement.
rationales.C:
Full statement: prohibiting recommendations of stock of companies that are clients of investment banking department is an ALTERNATIVE to firewalls, but PERMANENT prohibition would not be best answer because once an offering is complete and MNPI obtained by IB department becomes public, resuming publishing recommendations on the stock is not a violation of Code and Standards — the information no longer gives brokerage operation an advantage.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 2 Solution: 'Prohibiting recommendations of the stock of companies that are clients of the investment banking department is an alternative, but Option A states that this prohibition would be permanent, which is not the best answer. Once an offering is complete and the material nonpublic information obtained by the investment banking department becomes public, resuming publishing recommendations on the stock is not a violation of the Code and Standards, because the information of the investment banking department no longer gives the brokerage operation an advantage in writing the report.'

questions.stem: *Firewalls' review of employee/proprietary trading* *most likely*:

options.A: review of employee trading through the maintenance of 'watch,' 'restricted,' and 'rumor' lists is one of the elements of a firewall system.
options.B: Employee trading may not be monitored because personal accounts are private.
options.C: Only a single restricted list is used; watch and rumor lists are not part of firewall practice.

rationales.A:
Full statement: review of employee trading through the maintenance of 'watch,' 'restricted,' and 'rumor' lists is one of the elements of a firewall system; this creates layered categorization of securities under different levels of monitoring/restriction.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance on firewall elements: 'review of employee trading through the maintenance of "watch," "restricted," and "rumor" lists.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Reviewing employee and proprietary trading via list-based monitoring is a firewall element.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The guidance describes maintaining watch, restricted, AND rumor lists.

questions.stem: *Documentation of firewall procedures* *most likely*:

options.A: Documenting firewall procedures is discouraged because records could be discoverable in litigation.
options.B: documentation of procedures designed to limit flow of information between departments AND of the actions taken to enforce those procedures is an element of a firewall system.
options.C: Only the procedures themselves need documenting; enforcement actions may remain informal.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Documentation of both the procedures and their enforcement is an element of an effective firewall.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance on firewall elements: 'documentation of the procedures designed to limit the flow of information between departments and of the actions taken to enforce those procedures.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Actions taken to ENFORCE the procedures must be documented as well.

questions.stem: *Heightened proprietary trading review during MNPI* *most likely*:

options.A: Proprietary trading must halt entirely, without exception, whenever the firm holds any MNPI.
options.B: Proprietary desks may trade normally during MNPI possession if traders certify they have not seen it.
options.C: heightened review or restriction of proprietary trading while a firm is in possession of material nonpublic information is an element of a firewall system.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The element is HEIGHTENED REVIEW OR RESTRICTION — market-making and risk-arb contexts may warrant continued, scrutinized activity rather than a full stop.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Self-certification is not the control; heightened review or restriction of the activity is.
rationales.C:
Full statement: heightened review or restriction of proprietary trading while a firm is in possession of material nonpublic information is an element of a firewall system — recognizes that proprietary trading is high-risk area for MNPI misuse.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance on firewall elements: 'heightened review or restriction of proprietary trading while a firm is in possession of material nonpublic information.'

questions.stem: *Preventing selective disclosure by companies* recommended practices *most likely*:

options.A: encourage companies to issue press releases prior to analyst meetings and conference calls and to SCRIPT those meetings/calls to decrease chance further information will be disclosed.
options.B: Companies should be encouraged to hold analyst calls before issuing any press release, to reward attentive analysts.
options.C: Scripting analyst meetings is discouraged because it reduces the information content of the call.

rationales.A:
Full statement: encourage companies to issue press releases prior to analyst meetings and conference calls and to SCRIPT those meetings/calls to decrease chance further information will be disclosed; if material nonpublic information is disclosed for first time in analyst meeting or call, encourage company to promptly issue press release or otherwise make information publicly available.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Compliance Practices — Issue Press Releases: 'Members and candidates should encourage companies they follow to issue press releases prior to analyst meetings and conference calls and to script those meetings and calls to decrease the chance that further information will be disclosed. If material nonpublic information is disclosed for the first time in an analyst meeting or call, members and candidates should encourage the company to promptly issue a press release or otherwise make the information publicly available.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The recommended sequence is the reverse: press release FIRST, then the meeting or call.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Scripting is recommended precisely to reduce the chance that further material information slips out selectively.

questions.stem: *Documenting mosaic research* *most likely*:

options.A: Mosaic research files should be purged periodically to limit regulatory exposure.
options.B: although analysts are free to use mosaic information in their research reports, they should save and document all their research.
options.C: Only the final report need be retained; underlying notes and sources may be discarded.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Saving and documenting ALL the research is the protective practice.
rationales.B:
Full statement: although analysts are free to use mosaic information in their research reports, they should save and document all their research; evidence of the analyst's knowledge of public and nonmaterial nonpublic information about a corporation strengthens the assertion that the analyst reached his or her conclusions solely through appropriate methods rather than through using material nonpublic information.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Mosaic Theory guidance: 'Although analysts are free to use mosaic information in their research reports, they should save and document all their research. Evidence of the analyst's knowledge of public and nonmaterial nonpublic information about a corporation strengthens the assertion that the analyst reached his or her conclusions solely through appropriate methods rather than through using material nonpublic information.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The underlying pieces are exactly what demonstrate the mosaic was built from public and nonmaterial information.

questions.stem: *Acceptable forecasting practice* to avoid market manipulation *most likely*:

options.A: Acceptable practice is publishing a single point estimate to give clients an unambiguous forecast.
options.B: Model assumptions should be withheld as proprietary, with only conclusions disclosed.
options.C: acceptable practice is to write reports that (1) FRAME earnings projections in RANGE of possible outcomes and (2) OUTLINE CLEARLY the assumptions used in models that took into consideration relevant findings from research.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Framing projections as a RANGE of outcomes is the acceptable practice — a bare point estimate invites the manipulation concern.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Clearly OUTLINING the assumptions is the second half of the acceptable practice.
rationales.C:
Full statement: acceptable practice is to write reports that (1) FRAME earnings projections in RANGE of possible outcomes and (2) OUTLINE CLEARLY the assumptions used in models that took into consideration relevant findings from research; this contrasts with Example 5 Gordon's violation of exaggerating single-point projections to force company guidance updates.

Correct. Per Example 5 Comment (Gordon): 'However, it would have been acceptable for Gordon to write a report that (1) framed her earnings projection in a range of possible outcomes and (2) outlined clearly the assumptions used in her Hygene models that took into consideration the findings from her trip through Latin America.'

questions.stem: *Firm information barriers to contain MNPI* *most likely*:

options.A: per Example 2 (Peter/Bright Ideas): Peter's firm should have adopted information barriers to prevent the communication of nonpublic information between departments of the firm.
options.B: No structural remedy was needed; Peter's individual discretion should have sufficed.
options.C: The firm's only failing was not requiring salespeople to sign confidentiality agreements.

rationales.A:
Full statement: per Example 2 (Peter/Bright Ideas): Peter's firm should have adopted information barriers to prevent the communication of nonpublic information between departments of the firm; firm-level information barriers protect against personnel overhearing/observing MNPI during interdepartmental communications.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment (Peter): 'Peter's firm should have adopted information barriers to prevent the communication of nonpublic information between departments of the firm.' Also per Practice Problem 2 Solution: firewalls (physical and informational barriers) are the best policy to prevent violation of II(A).
rationales.B: Incorrect. The example's lesson is firm-level: information barriers (including physical separation) should prevent interdepartmental MNPI flow.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Agreements alone do not contain information; barriers between departments do.

questions.stem: *Compliance officer coordination for wall-crossing* *most likely*:

options.A: Wall-crossing may be arranged informally between the two employees involved.
options.B: if employee behind firewall believes he/she needs to share confidential information with someone on the other side, employee should consult designated compliance officer to determine whether sharing is necessary and how much information should be shared.
options.C: Once behind the firewall, an employee may never share information with the other side under any process.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Sharing across the wall must be coordinated through the DESIGNATED COMPLIANCE OFFICER, not arranged bilaterally.
rationales.B:
Full statement: if employee behind firewall believes he/she needs to share confidential information with someone on the other side, employee should consult designated compliance officer to determine whether sharing is necessary and how much information should be shared; if sharing is necessary, compliance officer should coordinate the process of 'looking over the wall' so that necessary information will be shared and integrity of procedure will be maintained.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance: 'If an employee behind a firewall believes that he or she needs to share confidential information with someone on the other side of the firewall, the employee should consult a designated compliance officer to determine whether sharing the information is necessary and how much information should be shared. If the sharing is necessary, the compliance officer should coordinate the process of "looking over the wall" so that the necessary information will be shared and the integrity of the procedure will be maintained.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Controlled wall-crossing exists — it simply requires compliance-officer consultation and supervision.

questions.stem: *Employers' restrictions or prohibitions on personal trading* *most likely* practices:

options.A: Employers may not monitor employees' personal trading, as that would invade financial privacy.
options.B: Personal-trading controls consist solely of an annual ethics attestation.
options.C: such practices may include: firm monitoring of personal trading, mandating periodic reports of personal transactions, and implementing a restricted list and watch list of companies.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Monitoring personal trading is a recognized employer practice for preventing MNPI misuse.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The practices include trade monitoring, periodic transaction reports, and restricted/watch lists — not a lone attestation.
rationales.C:
Full statement: such practices may include: firm monitoring of personal trading, mandating periodic reports of personal transactions, and implementing a restricted list and watch list of companies; combination of monitoring, reporting, and list-maintenance forms comprehensive personal trading compliance program.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Compliance Practices — Comply with Personal Trading Limitations: 'Such practices may include firm monitoring of personal trading, mandating periodic reports of personal transactions, and implementing a restricted list and watch list of companies.'
